query: poison causes bluish discoloration stomach postmortem

retrieved_docs.title: Scombroid and Histamine Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Signs and symptoms typically occur within 2 hours of ingesting a fish with Scombroid poisoning and last for about 12 to 48 hours. The most common symptoms are rash, flushing, headache, and diarrhea. Less common symptoms include abdominal cramps, blurred vision, cold-like sensation, dizziness, nausea, sweating, and tachycardia. Severe reactions cause angioedema, tongue swelling, respiratory distress, cardiac arrest, and death. Patients taking isoniazid or monoamine oxidase inhibitors can have much worse reactions because of the histaminase blockade in the gastrointestinal tract.

retrieved_docs.title: Bleach Toxicity(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Eyes: Commonly reddened, irritated, and tearing. Vision may be blurry. Mouth/throat: Irritation is normal to the examiner. Absent other caustic ingestants, the mouth, and throat are resistant to damage. Skin: Mild irritation may be evident. Do not let the patient spread the chemicals. Stomach/GI tract: These are rare symptoms. The GI tract is resistant and resilient. Respiratory: Irritation, including bronchospasm, can occur. This does not apply to chlorine gas exposure, a much more severe problem. Exposure to chlorine gas can be fatal. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Stages of Decomposition
retrieved_docs.content: The earliest external sign of putrefaction is the greenish discoloration of the skin of the anterior abdominal wall in the right iliac fossa region. In this region of the abdomen, the caecum, loaded with semi-solid intestinal content and commensal gut bacteria, lies quite superficial. This greenish discoloration of the skin results from the formation of sulfhemoglobin facilitated by the commensal intestinal bacteria that invade the tissues after death. This skin discoloration in the right iliac fossa region appears about 18 hours after death. In temperate climates, this greening may first appear 2 to 3 days after death. The ambient temperature affects the speed of onset of putrefaction and its rate of progression.

retrieved_docs.title: Bleach Toxicity(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Mouth/throat: In the home and emergency department, give plenty of water to drink. Milk may be more soothing but not necessarily. Stomach/GI tract: At home and in the emergency department, do not induce vomiting; if the bleached burned on the way down, it would burn on the way back up. Give plenty of water.

retrieved_docs.title: Comprehensive Review of Bioterrorism -- Issues of Concern -- Symptoms and Signs
retrieved_docs.content: Abrin is significantly more toxic than ricin. The estimated fatal dose is 0.1 to 1 microgram per kilogram body weight. Poisoning occurs following ingestion of seeds or contamination of food or water. It presents as abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, hemorrhagic gastritis, and renal failure. Following inhalation, pulmonary edema can occur. Cerebral edema, convulsions, and CNS depression can also occur. Death can occur within 72 hours of exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: Ackee Fruit Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Acute gastritis Acute poisoning (general) Aspirin poisoning Hypoglycemia Influenza Intestinal obstruction Meningitis Sulfonylurea poisoning

retrieved_docs.title: Caustic Ingestions -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with caustic ingestion may be asymptomatic in one extreme or develop severe manifestations in another. Vomiting, drooling, chest pain, abdominal pain, and breathing and swallowing difficulty may be reported. Hematemesis may be caused by blood vessel erosion.

retrieved_docs.title: Bleach Toxicity(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Ammonia toxicity Acute respiratory distress syndrome Smoke inhalation injury Hydrogen sulfide inhalation

retrieved_docs.title: Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Classification of Post Mortem Changes
retrieved_docs.content: The appearance of postmortem staining depends on multiple factors, including, amount of blood at the time of death and the coagulability status, etc. Lividity is feeble/ less marked in deaths following blood loss/ in anaemics, while it is more intense in cases of asphyxia deaths. Post mortem staining can be of a distinct color in cases of poisoning. Cherry red staining presents in carbon monoxide poisoning, pink-colored staining is observable in cyanide poisoning, and brown colored staining occurs in nitrate poisoning. [28] [29] [30] Thus, postmortem staining, besides PMI, can provide useful information on the position of the body, or even the cause of death. Late post mortem changes: Autolysis:

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Decomposition
retrieved_docs.content: Unlike autolytic changes, putrefactive changes are visible on a macroscopic level as discoloration of the skin or bloating of body parts such as the face, abdomen, breast, and scrotum. It can appear in various forms, such as putrefactive fluids and putrefactive gases.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Stages of Decomposition
retrieved_docs.content: Active decay is a stage where putrefaction accelerates after bloating. Postmortem purging where putrefactive body fluids become forced out of body orifices is observable during this stage of decomposition. The detachment of hair or hair sloughing and black discoloration of ruptured skin are seen.

retrieved_docs.title: Deadly Single Dose Agents -- Function -- Symptoms/Physical Findings/Labs/EKG
retrieved_docs.content: Caustics/household products: drooling, perioral burns, respiratory distress, dysphagia, metabolic effects (methemoglobinemia, hemolysis, cyanide toxicity)

retrieved_docs.title: Dakin Solution -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Ingesting diluted sodium hypochlorite generally causes only mild stomach irritation; however, swallowing larger amounts can cause more serious symptoms, including chest pain, delirium, hypotension, burns to the gastrointestinal tract, shock, bradycardia, nausea, and vomiting. [10] The concentration of sodium hypochlorite in industrial-strength bleach is high and may cause severe injury. [8] Never mixing ammonia with sodium hypochlorite (bleach or bleach-containing products) is important. This mixture can produce toxic chlorine gas that can cause choking and serious breathing problems. HCl is formed when chlorine gas contacts moist tissues like the eyes or lungs. HCl is a digestive juice that damages tissue; it can damage the airways, cause asphyxiation, and result in death. It is important to note that activated charcoal does not effectively treat (adsorb) sodium hypochlorite. Treatment depends on the severity of the toxicity with medical management for mild-moderate cases of toxicity to surgery if there are perforations noted along the gastrointestinal tract.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Stages of Decomposition
retrieved_docs.content: In the bloated stage, body parts, including organs and soft tissues, swelling due to the accumulation of putrefactive gases or other decompositional products from the putrefaction process. It usually starts in the abdomen and then slowly affects other parts, including the face, breasts, and genitals. [11] [12] Also, during this stage, skin changes occur, such as blisters and slippage. Skin slippage at the extremities is known as degloving. Moreover, the phenomenon of marbling is also present during this stage, where blood vessels are visible on the skin as greenish-black streaks and eventually results in skin discoloration ranging from green to black. [4] These postmortem changes are apparent in about 24 to 48 hours after death.

retrieved_docs.title: Alkali Toxicity(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who have ingested alkali substances can exhibit various symptoms, whereas those who have ingested benign substances or in smaller quantities will often remain asymptomatic. However, individuals with more significant ingestions often experience symptoms, including abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, hematemesis, bloody stools, dysphagia, odynophagia, and chest pain. If there has been pulmonary exposure, individuals may experience shortness of breath.

retrieved_docs.title: Meperidine -- Toxicity -- Signs and Symptoms of Overdose
retrieved_docs.content: Patients can have shallow or no breathing, signs of cyanosis like blue lips or fingernails, fatigue, convulsion, low blood pressure, bradycardia, constipation, stomach cramps, nausea and vomiting, cold and clammy skin, drowsiness, lightheadedness, and twitching muscles. In severe overdose, patients may experience circulatory relapse, cardiac arrest, apnea, and, in some cases, death.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2692
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “chemically induced” can be applied to the conditions قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), خلل الحركة المتأخر (delayed motility disorder), فطار غزوي (aggressive breakfast), and تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion). In particular, “chemically induced” is an allowed qualifier for تهديد بالإجهاض, indicating that this form of threatened abortion may arise as a result of chemical induction. Recognizing the chemically induced nature of these entities can aid clinicians in identifying underlying causes and tailoring appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.content: Asphyxiation and strangulation are medical events that are classified under the category of other injuries and conditions due to external causes, indicating that they arise from external mechanisms rather than internal disease processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2256

This relationship indicates that any disorder classified under “Disorder due to and following poisoning” can include cases where the toxic agent is a non‑medicinal substance, helping clinicians to organize and address poisoning‑related health issues more precisely.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1828
retrieved_docs.content: The conditions نوبة (seizure), خلل الحركة المتأخر (late‑onset movement disorder), فطار غزوي, intestinal infection, and إجهاض تلقائي غير كامل (incomplete spontaneous abortion) are all recognized as entities that can be qualified as chemically induced. Identifying a chemical trigger as a possible cause is important for the evaluation and management of these diverse clinical presentations, because exposure to certain substances may precipitate seizures, movement abnormalities, gastrointestinal infections, and incomplete abortions. Awareness of the chemically induced nature of these conditions helps clinicians consider appropriate diagnostic testing, preventive strategies, and targeted therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1627
retrieved_docs.content: The toxic effect of an unspecified substance, especially one that is non‑medical in origin (التأثير السام لمادة غير محددة، خاصة غير الطبية من حيث المصدر) is classified as a subcategory of metal poisoning (التسمم بالمعادن). This relationship indicates that exposures to unidentified non‑medical agents can be understood within the broader framework of metal poisoning, which encompasses various toxic effects caused by metals and guides clinical assessment and management.

For each organ system—respiratory and thoracic specimens, male and female genital specimens, gastrointestinal and abdominal specimens, and other organs and tissues—there is a general parent category describing “abnormal findings in specimens of … ; abnormal levels of substances usually of non‑medical origin.” Each of these general categories in turn has a more specific child category denoting “non‑specified abnormal finding” for that organ system. This hierarchical structure links detailed laboratory observations to broader categories of external‑cause injury. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 5 -- Community 2951
retrieved_docs.content: In enzymology, the concept of an “Allowed qualifier” is applied to the conditions قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), التسمم الوشيقي (botulism poisoning) and تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 323
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Disorder due to and following poisoning” serves as a broad classification that encompasses both general poisoning (التسمم) and poisoning by non‑medicinal substances. Within this framework, the specific condition of alcohol poisoning (التسمم الكحولي) is recognized as a distinct subtype of poisoning. The subcategory “Poisonings and Drug Effects” is also placed under the umbrella of poisoning, indicating that drug‑related toxic effects are considered part of the overall poisoning spectrum. Clinical observations link poisoning (التسمم) with adrenal syndrome (متلازمة الأدرينالية), highlighting an association that may be relevant for diagnosis and management of patients presenting with toxic exposures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 791
retrieved_docs.content: substance detections—whether heavy metals, cocaine, or other non‑medical chemicals—in samples from the respiratory tract, male and female genitalia, gastrointestinal tract, or other tissues are all classified as external‑cause injuries and are further refined into more specific subcategories. The category “Other injuries and conditions due to external causes” classifies itself, reinforcing its role as a top‑level grouping for these findings. Additionally, “Asphyxiation and strangulation” is explicitly classified as an “Other injuries and conditions due to external causes,” linking a traumatic mechanism to the same external‑cause framework. Overall, the knowledge base organizes diverse abnormal laboratory findings and traumatic events under a unified external‑cause classification, providing a structured approach for clinicians to interpret and manage injuries or conditions that arise from external exposures or forces.
